messages.content: Backtest a 50/200 MA golden-cross on the top 50 stocks.

I'll run a classic 50/200 golden-cross on the top 50 stocks. Assumptions: full data range (2019–2026), long-only golden-cross entries / death-cross exits, no fixed stop or target (pure MA rule), default capital and position sizing.Here are the real results for the 50/200 SMA golden-cross, top-50 universe, 2019–2026:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 204 |
| Win rate | 54.9% |
| Total return | +281.3% |
| CAGR | 22.3% |
| Max drawdown | -43.75% (₹4,37,500) |
| Net P&L | ₹28,12,900 on ₹10L capital |

**Honest read:**

- **Solid headline numbers.** 22% CAGR over ~7.5 years and a >50% win rate on a trend-following rule is respectable. Note that in a trend system, win rate isn't the point — the winners running far outweigh many small losers.
- **The drawdown is the catch.** A -43.75% peak-to-trough is brutal. The 50/200 cross is a *slow* signal: it enters well after a bottom and exits well after a top, so you routinely give back a big chunk of gains before the death cross fires. You'd need real conviction to sit through a ~44% drawdown.
- **Sample is decent** (204 trades), so it's not a fluke of one or two lucky picks, but 2019–2026 was largely a bull regime for Indian equities, which flatters any long-only trend follower.

**Worth testing next:**
- Add a `trail_stop_pct` or `stop_loss_pct` to cut that drawdown — the raw MA exit is the main weakness.
- Compare against a faster combo (e.g. 20/100) to see the speed/drawdown trade-off.

Want me to sweep a stop/trail level to see if we can tame the -44% drawdown without killing the return?
Run an RSI mean-reversion on the top 40, buy under 30, exit at 55.

I'll run RSI mean-reversion on the top 40 stocks. Assumptions: standard 14-period RSI, buy when RSI crosses below 30, exit when it recovers past 55, same 2019–2026 range, default capital/sizing, no fixed stop or hold cap.Here are the real results — RSI(14) mean-reversion, top 40, buy <30 / exit >55, 2019–2026:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 399 |
| Win rate | 71.9% |
| Total return | +237.3% |
| CAGR | 17.8% |
| Max drawdown | -46.61% (₹4,66,100) |
| Net P&L | ₹23,72,700 on ₹10L capital |

**Honest read:**

- **High win rate, as expected.** 71.9% is classic mean-reversion behavior — you win often, in small amounts, buying dips that bounce. The flip side is the losers tend to be large (a beaten-down stock that keeps falling), so the win rate overstates how "safe" it is.
- **Return is a touch lower than the MA cross (17.8% vs 22.3% CAGR)** but with far more trades (399 vs 204) — more activity, more transaction cost exposure, more chances for the edge to hold up.
- **Drawdown is again the problem: -46.6% (₹4.66L).** Mean-reversion's Achilles' heel is exactly this — no stop means you keep holding names that break down, and in a market-wide selloff *every* position drifts down together. There's no exit rule here except "RSI recovers to 55," which may never come for a genuinely broken stock.

**The real weakness:** buying oversold with no stop and no max-hold means a few catastrophic holds drive that drawdown. This strategy almost *demands* a stop-loss or a max_hold_days cap.

Want me to sweep a `stop_loss_pct` (or add `max_hold_days`) to see if we can cut that ~47% drawdown while keeping the high win rate? That's the single most useful next test here.
Test the Supertrend strategy on the top 30.

I'll run Supertrend on the top 30 stocks. Assumptions: standard ATR(10) with 3× multiplier, long on bullish flip / exit on bearish flip, 2019–2026, default capital/sizing, no extra fixed stop or target.Here are the real results — Supertrend, ATR(10) × 3, top 30, 2019–2026:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 746 |
| Win rate | 44.4% |
| Total return | +256.6% |
| CAGR | 18.6% |
| Max drawdown | -27.47% (₹2,74,700) |
| Net P&L | ₹25,66,200 on ₹10L capital |

**Honest read:**

- **Best drawdown of the three so far.** -27.5% (₹2.74L) vs the MA cross (-₹4.38L) and RSI (-₹4.66L). Supertrend's ATR-based exit flips out of downtrends faster, so it doesn't sit through the deep gives-back — that's the real win here.
- **Low win rate is normal and fine.** 44.4% is typical trend-following — lots of small false-flip losers, paid for by a handful of big trend winners. The return proves the math works.
- **But 746 trades is a lot.** That's the flip side of a fast signal: whipsaw. Transaction costs matter a great deal here — I used the default `cost_pct`, and if real-world slippage/brokerage is higher than modeled, this high-churn strategy loses the most edge of the three. Worth stress-testing with a higher `cost_pct`.
- **Smaller universe (30 vs 40/50)** means more concentration — the results lean more on fewer names, slightly higher single-stock risk.

**Scoreboard across your three equity tests:**

| Strategy | CAGR | Max DD (₹) | Trades | Win% |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MA 50/200 (top50) | 22.3% | -4.38L | 204 | 54.9% |
| RSI <30/>55 (top40) | 17.8% | -4.66L | 399 | 71.9% |
| **Supertrend (top30)** | **18.6%** | **-2.75L** | 746 | 44.4% |

Supertrend gives the best return-per-unit-of-drawdown of the bunch. The main risk to verify is cost sensitivity given the high trade count.

Want me to (a) re-run Supertrend with a higher `cost_pct` to check whipsaw robustness, or (b) sweep the `multiplier` (e.g. 2 / 3 / 4) to see if fewer, cleaner flips improve it?
